turns.user: with given s_theta(x,t), the trajectories are samples from rho(x,t), with these two information can we have a consistency loss ?
turns.assistant:
Yes — **implicit score matching** (Hyvärinen 2005). If $s_\theta$ is the true score of the particle density, then:

$$\mathcal{L}_{\text{ISM}}(t) = \mathbb{E}_{x \sim \rho_\theta}\!\left[\tfrac{1}{2}|s_\theta(x,t)|^2 + \nabla_x \cdot s_\theta(x,t)\right]$$

is minimized. Evaluated on particles:

$$\mathcal{L} = \frac{1}{MK}\sum_{i,k}\left[\tfrac{1}{2}|s_\theta(x_i^k, t_k)|^2 + \text{div}\, s_\theta(x_i^k, t_k)\right]$$

**Why this is attractive:**
- No $F$ computation at all — no $F^{-T}$ solve,...
turns.note: Novel loss function ideation: combining particle samples with learned score for self-consistency.

turns.user: but for grid-based score, how can one compute s_target ?

You're right to ask — the target computation is the same regardless of representation.

3. $s_{\text{target}} = F^{-T}[s_0 - \nabla_{x_0}\ln|\det F|]$

The self-referential loop is unchanged. Grid vs neural network only changes how $s$ is stored and differentiated — not how the target is computed.
